title: Chapter 14: The Double Agent
content: The fluorescent lights of Sterling Capital hum at a frequency I've come to associate with purpose.

I sit at my desk, fingers hovering over the keyboard, and for the first time since this whole thing started, I know what I'm doing. Not surviving. Not reacting. Not squeezing advantage out of thin air like some multiverse day trader. I have a plan.

Julian C — the Julian in Clonakilty, the one with shadows in his eyes and a frame job hanging over his head — mentioned private contractors. Military records. Networks of people who operate in the space between official and off-the-books. I couldn't chase those threads in Ireland. I was too busy trying to keep him from running. But here, in Timeline A, I have something better than a rusty laptop and spotty WiFi. I have Sterling Capital's research infrastructure, my own cleared browser history, and a Fork app that lets me cross-reference realities.
I'm not cheating for myself anymore.
I'm cheating for someone else.
It feels different. It feels like the first real thing I've done since the glitching started.

I open the Fork app. The interface is familiar now — the branching paths, the soft pulse at the edges when timelines are close. I've been using it wrong. Using it small. Pricing errors and bid numbers and — god, I was such a coward. The multiverse wasn't giving me a cheat code for quarterly reports. It was giving me a goddamn search engine that spans reality.

I start typing. Military contractors. Ireland. Discharge anomalies. The search results in Timeline A are sparse — public records, a few LinkedIn profiles of people who've scrubbed their histories clean. But I can feel the shape of something underneath. I just need to find it in a timeline where the dirt is closer to the surface.
My phone buzzes. An all-floor email from the executive committee: MERIDIAN DEAL — CODE RED. ALL ANALYSTS ON DECK.

Right. The Meridian deal. The one where I helped Julian A steal a margin point from a reality where he'd already figured it out. Where I was useful and clever and exactly the kind of person Sterling Capital rewards.
The guilt doesn't hit the way it used to. I think that should worry me more than it does.

The floor is electric with controlled panic. People cluster around monitors, voices pitched low and urgent. The Meridian acquisition — Sterling's bid for the healthcare analytics firm that was supposed to be the crown jewel of Q3 — has been compromised. Someone leaked the bid strategy. Not just the numbers, but the whole playbook: the ceiling price, the walk-away point, the regulatory concessions they were prepared to offer.
Meridian's board adjusted their counter-offer within twenty minutes of the leak hitting. Surgical. Like someone handed them the answers to the test.
I know what that feels like. I've been that someone.

Julian's office door is closed. The blinds are drawn. I can see the silhouette of the executive committee inside — Henderson, Park, Wheeler — pacing like sharks in a tank. When the door finally opens, they file out with the kind of grim faces that precede blood sacrifices.

Julian stands in the doorway. His sleeves are rolled to the forearms, tie loosened, and there's a tension in his jaw that I've only seen once before — the night he found the pricing irregularity and realized it wasn't a mistake. His eyes scan the floor, find me, and lock.
"Chen. My office."
I'm already moving before I can think about it. Leather chair, Manhattan skyline, him on the other side of the desk. We keep ending up here.
He doesn't sit. He leans against the window frame, arms crossed, and the city sprawls behind him like a threat.
"Someone on this floor gave Meridian our strategy. Not the broad strokes — the granular stuff. The walk-away number." He says it flat, no preamble. "I need you to find the leak."
His eyes hold mine. "You have the best instincts on this floor."

He doesn't say because you seem to know things before they happen. But it hangs in the air between us like smoke, like the copper aftertaste of a glitch. He's noticed. Maybe not the full scope of it — he couldn't have — but he's noticed something. The way I anticipate. The way I'm always half a step ahead.
"I'll do it," I say.

And just like that, I'm the double agent. Investigating a leak while being the biggest leak in the building — across reality. I'm going to hunt for someone who sold out Sterling Capital's secrets while I'm busy downloading secrets from other universes. The irony isn't lost on me. It sits in my chest like swallowed glass.
Julian nods once. "Report directly to me. Not Henderson. Not Park. Me."
"Why me?"
"Because you're the only person on this floor I'm not already fifty percent sure did it."
It's a compliment wrapped in an accusation wrapped in trust. I take it. That's all I seem to do these days — take things I haven't earned.

Lunch comes and I don't eat. I find the supply closet on the fourteenth floor that's become my shifting spot — abandoned, quiet, smelling of toner cartridge and industrial carpet. I close my eyes. Breathe. Reach.

The shift comes easier now, like a muscle I've been training. The fluorescent hum of Sterling fades and I'm somewhere else — softer light, the smell of butter and espresso, the distant sound of a street musician playing accordion.
Paris. Timeline B. Spring.

Avery B's apartment is in the Marais — exposed brick, tall windows, a tiny balcony with geraniums. The Eiffel Tower is visible if you lean out far enough, which I never do. Paris is beautiful in a way that makes me angry, because I can't afford to notice it. Not today.

I sit at Avery B's laptop. Different browser, different bookmarks, different search histories. Different newspapers and forums and databases that Timeline A Avery would never think to check. That's the point. That's the whole point.
I search: private military contractor, Ireland, special forces discharge, whistleblower retaliation.

The results are different here. In Timeline B, something happened — a journalist in Dublin spent three years investigating defense contracts before their paper spiked the story. The drafts are still archived online, rescued from the memory hole by some anonymous poster with a grudge and a server.
Osprey Solutions.

The name hits me like a dropped weight. Julian C said — "private contractors" — without naming them. But the way he said it, the way his voice dropped on the second word like he was swallowing something sharp. Osprey Solutions. Private military contractor. Dublin headquarters, operations across Ireland and the UK. Staffed by ex-military personnel with suspiciously clean discharge papers.

I read deeper. Board members with connections to defense ministries. Contracts that were never publicly bid. A former employee who filed a complaint and then withdrew it forty-eight hours later, then moved to Portugal and stopped answering emails.

I bookmark everything. Documents, cached pages, the journalist's drafts. I email the links to an address only Timeline A Avery knows about — a little trick I set up last week, crossing my own streams just enough to pass information between worlds.
The laptop screen blurs at the edges. I'm pushing my luck. I need to go back.
I shift.

Manhattan again. The supply closet. My hands are shaking, which they only do when I've been under too long. I make it back to my desk without anyone noticing, which is getting harder. The afternoon sun slants through the windows and I should be working on the Meridian leak investigation but I can't. Not yet. Not when Osprey Solutions is still burning a hole in my research queue.

I pull up the files I forwarded from Timeline B. Open them on my Sterling-issued laptop, which is monumentally stupid — using company equipment to research military contractors involved in an apparent frame job in another timeline. But I've passed monumentally stupid several exits ago.

The printouts come next. Our floor printer sits in a glass-walled room that nobody monitors after six, and I feed it page after page: Osprey's corporate filing, the journalist's unpublished investigation, the list of board members and their suspiciously parallel career trajectories. Paper accumulates around me like evidence at a crime scene, which — technically — it is. The warm smell of fresh toner rises from each sheet as I stack them, chemical and faintly sweet.
I'm reading about a former Osprey operative named Declan Harker when the glitch hits.
It's not a gentle shift. It's not a drift or a slide or a fade. It's a goddamn freight train.
Julian B's voice — Paris Julian, wine-and-museums Julian — cuts through the Manhattan air like he's standing directly behind me. Clear as day. Warm as breath.
"When you're ready, I'll still be here."

I grab the edge of my desk. The world tilts. Copper floods my mouth — hot, immediate, unmistakable — and then I feel it running from my nose, over my lip, dripping onto the Osprey printout in a perfect red circle.
"Oh my god — Avery?"
Danielle from Accounts Payable. Two desks over. Her eyes are wide, lunch container frozen halfway to her mouth.
I swipe my sleeve across my face. The cotton comes away smeared. "Dry air. Happens all the time."
"You're bleeding — "

"It's fine." I'm already typing again, pulling up a different tab, angling my screen away from her. The blood keeps coming — I can taste it, iron and salt — but I don't stop. I can't stop. The information is more important than the symptom.
Danielle stares for another eight seconds. Then she looks away. Because that's what people do. They look away.
I wipe my nose again. The blood is slowing. The printout beneath my hand has a red bloom on it now like a signature, like the multiverse signing its work.
INTEGRITY: 61%.
I don't look at the number. I just keep typing.

Eight PM. The floor has emptied out the way it always does — analysts fleeing for happy hours and apartments and lives that don't involve corporate espionage or interdimensional data smuggling. The late-office quiet settles over everything, thick as dust, broken only by the distant hum of the cleaning crew vacuum on another floor and the soft clatter of my keyboard, keys clicking in patterns that spell out someone else's crime.

I'm surrounded by paper. Osprey Solutions, Osprey Solutions, Osprey Solutions. The more I read, the more the shape of it becomes clear — a company that exists to do the things governments can't be seen doing, staffed by people who learned their trade in uniform and then discovered the private sector pays better. And somewhere in that architecture of plausible deniability, someone decided Julian C needed to take a fall.

I don't hear Julian A approach. I'm too deep in a board member's LinkedIn history — a man named Richard Calloway whose military record has a three-year gap and whose current title at Osprey is "Director of Special Projects," which is the kind of euphemism that should come with a body count warning.
"Avery."
I flinch. Slam the laptop shut. Papers scatter.

Julian A stands in the island of my desk lamp's light, tie finally gone, top button undone, looking like he's been running the same marathon I have. His eyes move across the printouts that surround me — the ones about the Meridian leak are conspicuously absent, replaced entirely by Osprey documentation.

He picks up a page. The paper is still warm from the printer, crisp between his fingers. Reads it. His expression shifts — confused first, as his brain tries to reconcile why a healthcare analytics bid leak has produced research on private military contractors. Then calculating. That familiar shift behind the eyes, the one I've learned to recognize as Julian assembling pieces that don't quite fit yet.
"Why are you researching military contractors?"
Panic hits like a second glitch. My mind races — excuses, deflections, lies. I've gotten so good at those. Too good.
"I have a — friend — who was in the military." The word friend comes out uneven. "I'm trying to help them."
Close enough to true. True enough to feel like lying.

Julian A doesn't move. He studies my face with an intensity that makes me want to look away, with the same focus he brings to term sheets and valuation models, except this time the thing he's assessing is me. My split ends. The bloodstain I missed on my sleeve. The way my hands are pressed flat against the desk to stop them shaking.
Then he does something I don't expect.

He pulls up a chair. The leather creaks as he sits down across from me, knees almost touching mine in the narrow space between desks. Not behind the desk — the position of authority. In front of it. The position of — what? Equals? Confidants?
"Tell me about your friend."
Not demanding. Offering.

I should lie. I should construct something clean and plausible — a university friend, a veterans' charity, a research project. I've done it a hundred times. A thousand. Across timelines and realities and every version of this conversation I've ever had.
Instead, I tell him something close to the truth.

"Someone I know was framed for something they didn't do." My voice is quiet. The late-office silence absorbs it like cotton absorbs blood. "The people who did it are still out there. Operating. Getting away with it. And I don't know how to prove it yet, but I'm — I'm trying."

I don't say multiverse. I don't say Fork app. I don't say there's a version of you in Ireland who can't sleep and a version in Paris who pours wine like an apology and a version whose life was destroyed by the same people in these printouts.

Julian A listens. He doesn't interrupt. He doesn't ask follow-up questions designed to poke holes in my story. He just listens, the way nobody has listened to me since this started — not Mika, not any version of myself.
When I finish, the silence stretches between us. Then he says:
"The best way to catch people who think they've gotten away with something is to make them think they're still getting away with it."

He says it like corporate strategy — like he's back in the Meridian war room, plotting counter-moves against whoever leaked their bid. And it is that. It's exactly that. But it's also more. It's advice that scales up and down, from boardrooms to — whatever this is. To framed soldiers and private contractors and women who cheat across reality.
Dual-use Julian. Always more than one application.
"Let them overextend," he continues. "Let them believe their cover is intact. People who've gotten away with something long enough start to think they're invincible. That's when they get sloppy."

He's looking at the Osprey printouts. But I feel, with a certainty I can't explain, that he's also looking at me. And I wonder if he sees the same thing — someone who's been getting away with something for so long that I've started to feel invincible too.

I look at him across the desk, this man who made me find pricing errors in database after database, who's now sitting three inches from my knees offering me strategy for a fight he doesn't even know the full scope of. Surrounded by printouts about crimes in another world. And I wonder if I've ever been this close to another human being without lying.
The answer is no. And I'm still lying now. Just — less.
My phone buzzes on the desk. The screen lights up with Mika's name.
"Brunch was Saturday. You weren't there. Again."

I turn the phone face-down. Julian A's eyes track the movement, then return to my face. He doesn't ask. The leather chair creaks as he leans back, and for a moment we just sit there — two people in an empty office, surrounded by paper trails to different kinds of crime, not saying all the things we're not saying.
INTEGRITY: 61%.
I'm using a multiverse to help one Julian. I'm making an ally of another. And I'm lying to every single one of them still — including myself.
The best way to catch people who think they've gotten away with something is to make them think they're still getting away with it.
I'm still getting away with it.
For now.
